impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder that conditions patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of the patent holder's unpatented products engages in unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, and whether the unpatented products constitute 'nonstaple goods' lacking substantial non-infringing uses that would shield the conduct from antitrust liability.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act. Ingevity conditioned licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' agreements to exclusively purchase Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the finding that the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses (e.g., in air-intake systems outside the patent's scope), defeating Ingevity's patent-based defense to antitrust liability.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors like BASF gain protection against incumbents who leverage patent portfolios to foreclose competition in adjacent unpatented product markets.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face significant antitrust exposure when structuring licensing agreements that bundle patented and unpatented products, particularly where the tied product has uses outside the patent's scope.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct in patent prosecution and on a Walker Process fraud claim for attempted monopolization based on a patent obtained through fraud on the PTO.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both inequitable conduct (granted to Global Tubing) and Walker Process fraud (granted to Tenaris), finding genuine disputes of material fact on both issues. Tenaris had acquired prior art documents (the CYMAX Documents) before prosecuting its coiled tubing patents, and questions remained about whether Tenaris intentionally withheld material prior art from the PTO and whether the conduct rose to Walker Process fraud supporting an antitrust claim.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent challengers benefit from the court's willingness to send both inequitable conduct and Walker Process fraud claims to trial, preserving the ability to seek antitrust damages for patents procured through fraud.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face heightened litigation risk when prior art was in their possession before prosecution, as courts will scrutinize the materiality and intent behind any failure to disclose such references to the PTO.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether OPM's regulatory writing requirement for overtime authorization under 5 C.F.R. § 550.111(c) is a valid exercise of rulemaking authority under 5 U.S.C. § 5542(a), which requires the government to pay for overtime 'officially ordered or approved.'
impacts.description: Sitting en banc, the Federal Circuit upheld OPM's longstanding regulation requiring written authorization for overtime pay for federal employees. The court held the writing requirement is a valid exercise of OPM's delegated rulemaking authority under the Federal Employees Pay Act. This resolves a major question about federal employee overtime compensation during emergencies like COVID-19.
impacts.positiveImplications: The federal government as employer benefits from the validation of its written-authorization requirement, limiting backpay exposure for unwritten overtime claims across agencies.
impacts.negativeImplications: Federal employees who worked significant unauthorized overtime (particularly during COVID-19) face a substantial barrier to recovering overtime pay absent written pre-authorization, even when supervisors were aware of the work.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether IHS registered nurses paid under Title 38's special salary provisions are entitled to enhanced compensation rates under Title 38 for nighttime, Sunday, holiday, and miscellaneous overtime work, or are limited to Title 5 rates.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit panel reversed dismissal of claims by IHS nurses seeking enhanced pay under 38 U.S.C. § 7453 for overtime, nighttime, Sunday, and holiday work performed during the COVID-19 pandemic. The court held the nurses adequately stated claims that IHS was required to apply Title 38 compensation provisions to eligible Title 5 healthcare employees, remanding for further proceedings on these pay enhancement claims.
impacts.negativeImplications: Federal healthcare agencies face potential backpay liability for failing to apply Title 38 enhanced compensation provisions to nurses and similarly situated healthcare employees, which could affect compensation practices across IHS and similar agencies.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Board governing the exercise of discretionary authority to decline institution of inter partes review proceedings constitute 'general statements of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions governing IPR non-institution decisions are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b). The court reasoned that because Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution and the Director retains ultimate decision-making authority, the instructions do not bind the agency and need not undergo formal rulemaking.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's maintained flexibility to decline IPR institution, preserving patent validity without the risk that procedural challenges could force broader IPR access.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that rely on IPR as a cost-effective mechanism to challenge patents (particularly technology companies facing frequent patent assertions) lose a procedural avenue to challenge discretionary non-institution policies that may reduce IPR availability.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming server-side tailoring of content presentation specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are directed to patent-ineligible abstract ideas under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's patents claiming systems for tailoring streamed content to a device's capabilities were ineligible under § 101, reversing the jury's infringement verdict in favor of GoTV. The court found the claims directed to the abstract idea of adapting content to device characteristics without inventive technological implementation. This resulted in reversal of a $2.5 million damages award.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies like Netflix gain stronger defenses against patent assertions covering common digital content delivery techniques, reinforcing § 101 as a meaningful barrier to broad software patents.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the digital content and streaming technology space face continued risk that patents directed to content adaptation and delivery optimization will be invalidated as abstract ideas under § 101.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether claims to a web conferencing system with time-shifting capabilities for asynchronous viewing of multimedia data streams are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed dismissal of a patent infringement suit against Google, finding the web conferencing patent claims ineligible under § 101. The patent covered systems allowing participants to view recorded portions of a web conference asynchronously. The ruling reinforces that broad claims to conventional computing techniques applied to web conferencing may be directed to abstract ideas.
impacts.positiveImplications: Major technology companies operating web conferencing platforms benefit from a narrowing of enforceable patent claims in this space, reducing licensing and litigation exposure.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent owners holding portfolios in web conferencing and collaboration technology face increased invalidity risk for claims that do not recite specific technical implementations beyond conventional computing.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in countervailing duty and antidumping investigations is consistent with the governing statute, and whether 19 U.S.C. § 1516a abrogates the common law right of public access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that the ITC's blanket practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential is incompatible with the governing trade statute and that § 1516a does not abrogate the common law right of access to judicial proceedings. The court affirmed the CIT's order directing the ITC to conform its confidentiality practices to the statute, which could increase transparency in trade remedy proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers, domestic competitors, and the public benefit from increased transparency in trade remedy proceedings, gaining potential access to information previously shielded from disclosure by the ITC's blanket confidentiality designations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that submit sensitive business data in response to ITC questionnaires in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations face increased risk that proprietary commercial information could be disclosed, as the ITC can no longer automatically designate all responses as confidential.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Restaurant Revitalization Fund statute (15 U.S.C. § 9009c) creates a money-mandating obligation enforceable under the Tucker Act, such that eligible applicants who applied but were not awarded grants in the statutory order may sue the government for damages.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that over 300 restaurants and businesses could proceed with Tucker Act claims against the United States for unpaid RRF grants. The court held that the statute's mandatory language requiring the SBA to award grants in the order applications were received created enforceable rights, and the SBA's failure to follow the statutory priority order gave rise to damages claims. This establishes that the RRF created individually enforceable rights for eligible applicants.
impacts.positiveImplications: Small businesses that applied for but were denied RRF grants due to the SBA's failure to process applications in the statutory order may pursue damages claims against the government, potentially recovering significant pandemic relief funds.
impacts.negativeImplications: The government faces substantial damages exposure from hundreds of unfunded RRF applicants, and the ruling may affect how agencies administer future grant programs with statutory priority requirements.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent holder's damages expert report in its entirety and then granting summary judgment based on absence of a remedy.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report and vacated summary judgment for Microsoft in a patent infringement case involving Azure cloud networking technology. The court found the blanket exclusion of the expert's opinions was an abuse of discretion, as the district court should have considered whether portions of the report could be remedied rather than excluding the entire report and then granting judgment on the basis that no damages evidence existed.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the ruling that district courts cannot use wholesale exclusion of damages evidence as a de facto merits disposition; courts must consider whether expert deficiencies can be corrected.
impacts.negativeImplications: Accused infringers like Microsoft lose a litigation strategy of seeking complete exclusion of damages evidence as a pathway to summary judgment; they must address infringement on the merits.

impacts.caseName: Coda Development s.r.o. v. Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether Goodyear was entitled to judgment as a matter of law overturning a jury verdict of trade secret misappropriation and $64 million in damages related to self-inflating tire technology, and whether correction of inventorship of a related patent was warranted.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed JMOL in favor of Goodyear, overturning a $64 million jury verdict ($2.8M compensatory + $61.2M punitive) for trade secret misappropriation under Ohio law. The court also affirmed denial of correction of inventorship. The ruling highlights the high evidentiary bar for maintaining trade secret claims when the asserted secrets are defined broadly and the evidence of misappropriation is challenged on sufficiency grounds.
impacts.positiveImplications: Large manufacturers benefit from the reinforcement that broadly defined trade secrets face rigorous scrutiny at JMOL, and that even substantial jury awards can be overturned when evidence of actual misappropriation is insufficient.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology developers and small innovators face heightened risk that trade secret claims, even those that survive trial and produce large jury verdicts, may be overturned on JMOL if the trade secrets are not precisely defined and the evidence of misappropriation is not independently compelling.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Board correctly construed 'encryption information' in claims directed to streaming partly encrypted media content, and whether the Board's obviousness determination based on that construction was supported by substantial evidence.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the Board's claim construction of a DivX patent covering streaming partly encrypted media content, vacated the Board's rejection of Netflix's obviousness challenge, and remanded for further proceedings. The decision clarifies the scope of claims relating to encryption in adaptive bitrate streaming systems, a technology central to modern content delivery.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming platforms benefit from narrower claim constructions that may render encryption-related media patents obvious over prior art, reducing licensing exposure in the streaming technology space.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the digital media encryption space face the risk that broader claim constructions adopted by the Board may be reversed, potentially leading to invalidation of patents covering foundational streaming encryption technologies.

impacts.caseName: Micron Technology Inc. v. Longhorn IP LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Circuit has appellate jurisdiction over an interlocutory bond order issued under Idaho's Bad Faith Assertions of Patent Infringement Act, and whether the Act is preempted by federal patent law.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit dismissed for lack of jurisdiction an appeal from an $8 million bond imposed under Idaho's Bad Faith Assertions of Patent Infringement Act against patent assertion entities Longhorn IP and Katana Silicon Technologies. While not reaching the merits of federal preemption, the court's treatment of the case signals the viability of state anti-patent-troll statutes as a tool to impose costs on entities making allegedly bad faith patent infringement assertions.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies targeted by patent assertion entities benefit from the continued viability of state bad faith patent assertion statutes as a deterrent and remedy against abusive patent enforcement practices.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent enforcement entities face the risk of substantial bond requirements and litigation costs under state anti-patent-troll statutes, which may chill legitimate patent enforcement when applied broadly.

impacts.caseName: Game Plan, Inc. v. Uninterrupted IP, LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether common law trademark rights acquired through an asset purchase agreement can establish priority over a registered mark in a Trademark Board cancellation proceeding under Section 2(d) of the Lanham Act.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the TTAB's cancellation of Game Plan's registered mark 'I AM MORE THAN AN ATHLETE' and dismissal of Game Plan's opposition to UNIP's marks. UNIP established priority through common law rights acquired via an asset purchase agreement that transferred both the mark and associated goodwill. The ruling confirms that properly structured trademark assignments with goodwill can defeat registered marks in Board proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies acquiring trademark rights through asset purchases benefit from confirmation that properly structured assignments transferring marks with associated goodwill establish enforceable priority rights, even against prior registrants.
impacts.negativeImplications: Trademark registrants who fail to develop and present evidence of their own use and goodwill risk cancellation when a party with earlier common law rights (whether acquired or organic) challenges the registration.

impacts.caseName: Wonderland Switzerland AG v. Evenflo Company, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court correctly upheld a jury's finding of patent infringement under the doctrine of equivalents for car seat patents, and whether the permanent injunction was properly tailored.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit largely affirmed a jury verdict finding Evenflo's convertible car seats infringed Wonderland's car seat patents under the doctrine of equivalents, while partially vacating and remanding on injunction scope issues. The case addresses important questions about the boundaries of doctrine-of-equivalents infringement in mechanical patent cases and the proper scope of permanent injunctions in the consumer products space.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in the consumer products space benefit from the affirmation that doctrine of equivalents remains a viable basis for infringement findings and injunctive relief, even for mechanical innovations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Manufacturers of consumer products face continued exposure to doctrine-of-equivalents claims and potential injunctive relief that could require product redesign or market withdrawal.
